summary: Zhuge Liang; the Jieting reckoning. Court-martials Ma Su despite their close friendship; executes him with weeping eyes — the famous *huī lèi zhǎn Mǎ Sù* ('weeping over the execution'). Pensions Ma Su's family. Then memorialises Liu Shan demoting himself three grades for the failure. Wei Yan, Wang Ping, and the rest receive promotions for sound performance. Ends preparing the Second Northern Expedition.

Ma Su; executed for losing Jieting. Goes calmly to death, asking that Zhuge Liang treat his family kindly. Zhuge Liang weeps as the sentence is carried out. The novel's defining scene about discipline overruling personal affection.
Wang Ping; promoted; the Jieting survivor whose testimony helps establish the facts.
Zhao Yun; commands the retreat rear-guard, brings every man home — Zhuge Liang praises him as the only commander to lose no men in the campaign. The 'old tiger' still in form.

Cao Xiu; commands the Wei eastern army; tricked by the Wu officer Zhou Fang's false defection (faux self-mutilation with cut hair); marches into Wu territory at Shiting and is annihilated by Lu Xun's ambush. Cao Xiu falls ill from shame and dies shortly after.

Zhou Fang; the Wu officer of the chapter's centerpiece. Cuts his own hair as proof of sincerity in his fake defection; lures Cao Xiu deep into Wu territory. The hair-cutting deception that wins Shiting.
Lu Xun; commands the Wu side at Shiting; obliterates Cao Xiu's army with the prearranged ambush.
Zhu Huan; with Lu Xun at Shiting; one of the three corps commanders.
Sun Quan; ratifies Zhou Fang's scheme; Shiting's victory consolidates Wu's central authority.
Man Chong; warns Cao Xiu against Zhou Fang's defection; overruled.
Jia Kui; brings Wei relief to Shiting after Cao Xiu's defeat; covers the retreat.
Sima Yi; in the Wei central command; sends Jia Kui to relieve Cao Xiu.
Hao Zhao; first major appearance — assigned to fortify Chencang against Zhuge Liang's expected Second Expedition. The next chapter's antagonist.
Cao Rui; in the Wei court.
Guo Huai; on the Wei front.
Zhang He; on the Wei front.
Sun Zi; on the Wei council.
Gu Yong; on Sun Quan's council.
Xu Sheng; on the Wu front.
Jiang Wei; with Zhuge Liang's army.
Jiang Wan; in Zhuge Liang's staff.
Fei Yi; in Zhuge Liang's staff; carries despatches to Liu Shan.
Deng Zhi; in Zhuge Liang's staff.
Zhang Bao; with the expedition — wounded in this chapter during a pursuit. Dies of the wound at chapter's end. The third of the four young generals (with Guan Xing) reduced to one.
Wei Yan; promoted after the campaign.
Zhuge Jin; on the Wu side.

Zhuge Liang; submits the *Second Memorial on Setting Out* (the *Hou Chu Shi Biao* — sometimes attributed to him, sometimes considered later) and launches the Second Northern Expedition. Besieges Hao Zhao at Chencang. Hao Zhao holds out brilliantly through every assault: cloud-ladders burned with fire-arrows; mantlets crushed with millstones; tunnels countermined with cross-tunnels. Zhuge Liang fails to take the city; falls back when Wei relief approaches.

Hao Zhao; the chapter's hero on the Wei side. Holds Chencang against Zhuge Liang's full siege with under 3,000 men through every assault method the Shu army can think up. The defender's masterclass; one of the novel's great unsung small-scale commands. Dies of illness in the next chapter, just as the siege ends.
Wang Shuang; senior Wei general; sent by Cao Zhen as advance commander. Killed by Wei Yan in an ambush during the Wei pursuit at chapter's end.
Wei Yan; ambushes and kills Wang Shuang on the retreat — the chapter's signature combat moment.
Cao Zhen; commands the Wei central army; sends Wang Shuang and others against Zhuge Liang.
Cao Rui; in the Wei court.
Cao Xiu; dies of his Shiting shame this chapter — recap of the last chapter's defeat.
Sima Yi; in the Wei command; reviewing Cao Zhen's deployments.
Zhang He; on the Wei front.
Guo Huai; on the Wei front.
Sun Li; on the Wei front.
Fei Yao; Wei officer; killed in the campaign.

Zhao Yun dies. The chapter's quiet tragedy. The great Zhao Zilong — last of the Five Tigers — succumbs to old age and falls ill; dies in his bed. Zhuge Liang weeps when the news arrives: 'Heaven has cut off one of my arms.' Sons Zhao Tong and Zhao Guang carry the news to court.
Zhao Tong; Zhao Yun's elder son; brings news of his father's death to court.
Zhao Guang; Zhao Yun's younger son; brings news of his father's death to court.
Zhang Bao; named in passing — already dead from last chapter's wounds.
Zhang Fei; named in passing in the Zhao Yun eulogy recalling the founding generation.
Guan Yu; named in passing in the eulogy.
Yang Yi; on Zhuge Liang's staff.
Lu Xun; on the Wu side.

Zhuge Liang; the Third Northern Expedition. Receives news of Hao Zhao's death; sends Wei Yan and Jiang Wei to take the now-undefended Chencang. Takes Wudu and Yinping commanderies. Then word arrives: Sun Quan has formally declared himself Emperor of Wu — the Three Kingdoms are now three formal monarchies. Zhuge Liang advises Liu Shan to ratify diplomatically rather than break the alliance.
Sun Quan; declares himself Emperor of Wu; era Huanglong year 1. The last of the three kings to take the imperial title; the Three-Kingdoms triad is now formal. Names Sun Deng as heir apparent.
Sun Deng; named heir apparent of Wu.

Zhuge Ke; first major appearance — Zhuge Jin's clever son; impresses Sun Quan with quick wit (the famous donkey episode where he saves his father's face by adding two characters to a label). The future regent of Wu.
Zhuge Jin; father of Zhuge Ke; in Sun Quan's court.
Chen Zhen; sent by Liu Shan as Shu's ratifying envoy to Sun Quan's new title.
Empress Wu; Sun Quan's empress at the Wu accession.
Sun Jian; named in passing as the founding ancestor at the imperial accession ceremony.
Sun Ce; named in passing as the founding brother at the accession.
Zhang Zhao; senior in the Wu court at the accession.
Gu Yong; senior in the Wu court at the accession.
Lu Xun; commander-in-chief of Wu armies.
Wei Yan; takes Chencang upon Hao Zhao's death; takes Wudu.
Jiang Wei; takes Yinping with Wei Yan.
Hao Zhao; dies of illness; the Chencang garrison falls.
Cao Zhen; on the Wei front; falls ill from the strain.
Sima Yi; on the Wei command; the rising star against Zhuge Liang.
Cao Rui; in the Wei court.
Zhang He; on the Wei front.
Guo Huai; on the Wei front.
Wang Shuang; named in passing — killed last chapter.
Sun Li; on the Wei front.
Man Chong; on the Wei front.
Zhang Liao; named in passing — long dead.
Zhang Hu; Zhang Liao's son; with the Wei force.
Chen Shi; Shu officer with the expedition.
Zhang Bao; named in passing — dead.
Yang Yi; on Zhuge Liang's staff.
Jiang Wan; in Liu Shan's court.

Zhuge Liang; the Fourth Northern Expedition continues. With Cao Zhen ill and Sima Yi commanding the Wei front, Zhuge Liang fights a chain of engagements — captures Wei officers; bests Sima Yi on the Wei River; advances on Qishan. The two great minds' set-piece manoeuvring.

Sima Yi; takes overall command of the Wei front from the ailing Cao Zhen. Pursues a defensive strategy of refusing battle; Zhuge Liang tries every trick to draw him out. The long game begins; Sima Yi's caution will eventually break Zhuge Liang at Wuzhang Plains by attrition.
Cao Zhen; ill; nominally still chief commander but Sima Yi is doing the work.
Cao Rui; in the Wei court.
Zhang He; under Sima Yi; the senior field commander.
Guo Huai; on the Wei front.
Sun Li; on the Wei front.
Liu Ye; on the Wei council.
Hua Xin; in the Wei court.
Yang Fu; in the Wei court.
Wang Su; in the Wei court.
Zhang Bao; named in passing — dead.
Zhang Fei; named in passing — long dead.
Dong Jue; in Liu Shan's court.
Fan Jian; in Liu Shan's court.
Fei Yi; in Liu Shan's court.

Zhuge Liang; the Fourth Expedition closes. A rumour planted in Chengdu by the eunuch Gou An — bribed by Sima Yi — makes Liu Shan recall Zhuge Liang home on a manufactured pretext. Zhuge Liang must withdraw mid-campaign with battle finally going his way. On the retreat he tricks Sima Yi into thinking the Shu army has been reinforced (the lit-and-doused camp-fires); pulls his army out intact. Returns to Chengdu, clears his name, exposes Gou An. The lost opportunity that Liu Shan's court has handed Wei.
Gou An; the chapter's villain. Shu officer who turns traitor at Sima Yi's bribery; spreads the Chengdu rumour that brings Zhuge Liang home. Caught at chapter's end.
Sima Yi; engineers the Gou An rumour; loses the chance to capture Zhuge Liang on the retreat when the firelight bluff fools him.
Cao Zhen; on the Wei front.

Li Yan; the chapter's other Shu villain — as co-regent he is responsible for supplying Zhuge Liang at the front; falsifies despatches about supply shortages and Wu invasions to cover his own logistic failures. Will be impeached and demoted next chapter. The civil-side counterweight to Zhuge Liang collapses.
Qin Liang; Wei officer; killed in the engagements.
Zhang Hu; with the Wei force.
Chen Shi; Shu officer with the expedition; bears the brunt of Wei pressure; executed by Zhuge Liang for disobeying battle orders.
Deng Zhi; in Liu Shan's court.
Du Qiong; in Liu Shan's court.
Jiang Wan; in Liu Shan's court.
Yang Yi; on Zhuge Liang's staff.
